coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on carbon dots and gold nanocluster encapsulated metal–organic framework for detection of cephalexin residues in milk
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jalili, Roghayeh
Irani-nezhad, Mahsa Haddad
Khataee, Alireza
Joo, Sang Woo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on in situ incorporation of both Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and Green emitting carbon dots (gCDs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) to analysis of Cephalexin (CFX). Under a single excitation wavelength of 400 nm, the sensor exhibits dual-emissions centered at 520 and 630 nm. The fluorescence of AuNCs (630 nm) is selectively quenched by CFX, whereas the fluorescence of gCDs (520 nm) remainsalmostconstant. The ratiometric fluorescence signal (F520/F630) of the prepared composite (gCDc/AuNCs @ ZIF-8) is linearly proportional to the concentration of CFX from 0.1 to 6 ng/mL with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.04 ng/mL, which is below the maximum residues limit (MRL) of 100 ng/mL set by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). Moreover, the designed sensing platform was successfully applied to detect CFX in the milk samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic framework
Gold nanoclusters
Green carbon dots
Ratiometric sensor
Cephalexin

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 ratiometric sensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is funded by the grant NRF-2019R1A5A8080290 of the National Research Foundation of Korea (S.W.J.).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Fluorescence spectrum of sole BSA@AuNCs (yellow curve) and AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructure (red curve) under excitation at 400 nm (The inset illustrates the digital image of (A) AuNCs, (B) AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructure illuminated by a UV lamp), (b) Fluorescence spectrum of gCDs (The inset illustrates the digital image of gCDs illuminated by a UV lamp), and (c) Fluorescence spectrum of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8.

(a) SEM image of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructures, (b) TEM image of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructures, and (c) The XRD patterns of the ZIF-8 (blue curve), gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 (orange curve) and stimulated ZIF-8 (red curve).
(a) Fluorescence spectra of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructures after adding the various concentrations of CFX, (b) calibration regression curve.
Quenching of fluorescence intensity of: a) sole AuNCs, b) gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 structure in the presence of 2 ng/mL CFX (pH:7, incubation time: 7 min).
CFX detection mechanism using the ratiometric gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 probe.
Comparison of different methods for CFX sensing.
Analytical results of the determination of CFX in milk samples using the proposed method.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescent probe based on carbon dots and gold nanocluster encapsulated metal–organic framework for detection of cephalexin residues in milk

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Advanced Water and Wastewater Treatment Processes, Department of Applied Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, 51666-16471 Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, Gebze Technical University, 41400 Gebze, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, South Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mechanical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a ratiometric fluorescent probe based on in situ incorporation of both Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and Green emitting carbon dots (gCDs) into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) to analysis of Cephalexin (CFX). Under a single excitation wavelength of 400 nm, the sensor exhibits dual-emissions centered at 520 and 630 nm. The fluorescence of AuNCs (630 nm) is selectively quenched by CFX, whereas the fluorescence of gCDs (520 nm) remainsalmostconstant. The ratiometric fluorescence signal (F520/F630) of the prepared composite (gCDc/AuNCs @ ZIF-8) is linearly proportional to the concentration of CFX from 0.1 to 6 ng/mL with a low detection limit (LOD) of 0.04 ng/mL, which is below the maximum residues limit (MRL) of 100 ng/mL set by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA). Moreover, the designed sensing platform was successfully applied to detect CFX in the milk samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Cephalexin (CLX) is an effective broad-spectrum antibiotic that is used widely in veterinary medicine either to prevent or treat epidemic diseases such as respiratory and urinary tract infections and bronchitis. However, the illegal use of CFX in the livestock industry leads to residues in foods of animal origin. Long-term use of food contaminated with antibiotic residues may give rise to serious public health hazards such as the appearance of antibiotic-resistant bacterial strains, mutagenicity, and carcinogenicity [1–3].

Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has set a maximum residues limit (MRL) for CFX in various foods. In bovine milk, MRL for CFX has been set at 100 ng/mL. Various techniques like electrophoresis [4], electrochemistry [5], chromatography [6], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [7], and fluorescence [8]. However, these methods are associated with expensive chemicals and instrumentation, sophisticated procedures, time-consuming sample preparation, and skilled technicians. In this field, developing sensitive and fast analytical methods is of great importance. Gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) which are described as nanostructures with a diameter smaller than 2 nm are typically composed of ten to a hundred gold atoms. Due to their strong quantum confinement and discrete energy levels, AuNCs exhibit strong fluorescence and good biocompatibility. These advantages make them promising alternatives to organic dyes in constructing sensing and imaging systems [9].

Fluorescent sensors have a short response time with high selectivity and sensitivity and have profound applications in clinical diagnostics and environmental monitoring. However, the majority of reported fluorescent sensors use a single wavelength as an analytical response making their performance susceptible to minute variations in instrument efficiency, probe concentration, and environmental conditions. Ratiometric fluorescence methods employ the changes in the ratio of two fluorescence intensities at two well-resolved wavelengths concerning variations as analytical signals to determine analyte concentration. This approach effectively eliminates background and environmental fluctuations, exhibiting a distinct improvement in the accuracy and reliability of the detection results [11].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), a novel category of porous material recognized by extensive porosity, enormous internal surface area, chemical, and thermal stability have appeared as very beneficial functional materials for a variety of applications. As one of the typical MOFs, the zeolite-imidazole framework (ZIF-8) is extensively applied in catalysis, biosensing, and bioimaging, etc.Recently researchers have confirmed that introducing guest nanomaterials with various shapes and functions into MOF is a crucial way to synthesize composites with improved performance features of the guest materials [12,13]. In this way, Wang and et al. [14] proposed covalent organic frameworks (COF) layers form on Uio-66 (MOF) to eliminate aggregation-caused quenching which is observed in COFs. The resulted composite was a multimission material of the enol and keto states of the COFs and UiO-66. In another work, by Li and et al. [15] Eu3+ ions were incorporated into NH2-UiO-66, which had multiband fluorescence emission that was derivative from Eu3+ and Zr- oxo or Eu- oxo (linker-to-cluster charge transfer). In this composite, luminescent property, porosity, and open sites of amine group have been combined which made it be a small molecules-dependent luminescence material. Based on the confinement effect of MOFs, metal nanoclusters (NCs) can be loaded into the pores of MOFs to form new nanomaterials with enhanced fluorescence intensity and stability of NCs. We reported previously that the confinement of AuNCs in the framework of ZIF-8 caused an enhancement of fluorescence intensity near 10-fold [9,10].

Herein, we designed a ratiometric sensor using ZIF-8 as a host scaffold loaded with AuNCs and green emitting carbon dots (gCDs) as gest. The synthesized dual emissive gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite was used for ratiometric determination of CFX antibiotic (Scheme 1 ). In the presence of CFX, the fluorescence intensity of the AuNCs decreased while the fluorescence intensity of gCDs remained unchanged. The ratio of fluorescence intensity at two well-resolved wavelengths was used as the sensing signal. The advantages of encapsulating in ZIF-8 and ratiometry techniques were investigated. The suggested ratiometric assay was successfully used to determine the CFX concentration in various milk samples.

Urea, citric acid, bovine serum albumin (BSA), Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, phosphoric acid, Tris-HCl buffer, acetonitrile, and 2-methylimidazole were obtained by Sigma-Aldrich. Glucose, NaOH, Tetrachloroauric (III) acid (HAuCl4) is obtained from Merck (Germany). CFX is supplied by Dana pharmaceutical Co (Iran).

The fluorescence measurements were performed on a Perkin Elmer LS45 fluorescence spectrometer (USA). The morphologies of AuNCs and gCDS were observed using a JEM-2200FS (Japan) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Mira3) was used to characterize the surface topography of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) value was calculated via nitrogen adsorption/desorption technique at 77 K using a Quantachrom C (USA) device.

gCDs were prepared via one-step hydrothermal procedure as described previously [16]. Briefly, 700 mg glucose was dissolved in the 7 mL of mixture of water and concentrated phosphoric acid (2:5). The solution was heated in an oven at 90 °C for 30 min. After cooling, large particles were removed by centrifuge at 6000 rpm for 30 min. Then the pH of obtained gCDs was adjusted with NaOH (1 mol/L).

Fluorescent BSA@AuNCs were prepared according to previous report [17]. Concisely, 10 mL of HAuCl4 solution (10 mmol/L) was mixed with 10 mL of BSA solution (50 mg/mL) under gentle stirring. After 10 min, the pH of the solution was altered to 11.5 using NaOH solution. Then, the solution was kept at 80 °C for 15 min. The color of the resultant solution altered from yellow to dark red suggesting the formation of AuNCs. The pH of the resultant AuNCs was adjusted at 7.0 and stored at 4 °C.

The gCDc/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructures were synthesized in accordance with our previously reported method [11]. First, 20 mL of 2-methylimidazole (16 mmol) solution was added to 20 mL of Zn (NO3)2·6H2O (2 mmol) solution and stirred vigorously. The resultant ZIF-8 nanoparticles were collected by centrifuge and rinsed with water several times. In the next step, 5 mL of AuNCs and 1 mL of gCDs suspension were added and stirred for 2 h. Obtained gCDc/AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite was collected and rinsed with water and then dispersed in 100 mL deionized water.

Typically, 200 µL of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 suspension and 300 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution (0. 1 mol/L, pH 7.5) were added to a test tube with a plugin turn. Then, an appropriate volume of CFX standard or sample solution was added and the final volume of the test solution was reached to 5.0 mL with water. The prepared test solutions were kept 10 min at room temperature and then they were introduced into the quartz cell (1 cm × 1 cm) in turn for ratiometric fluorescence measurement. The excitation wavelength was set to 400 nm, and the fluorescence spectra were obtained in the 500–700 nm wavelength span.

Bovine milk samples (Raw and pasteurized) were obtained from local markets. For protein removal, 1.0 mL of acetonitrile was added into a 1.0 mL portion of each milk sample. After overtaxing for 20 s, the sample was centrifuged and the supernatant was collected and spiked by the addition of proper volumes of the CFX stock solution. The obtained samples were diluted 10 times using the Tris-HCl buffer solution (0. 1 mol/L, pH 7.5). Then the concentration of CFX was determined by the developed probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 ratiometric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: BSA@AuNCs were fabricated by a one-pot procedure using BSA as a reducing and capping agent. Under 400 nm excitation, the BSA@AuNCs showed faint luminescence emission at 630 nm (Fig. 1 ). By embedding the BSA@AuNCs into ZIF-8 cavities, their fluorescence intensity increased about 8-fold. This enhancement is attributed to the fascinating restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM) induced emission enhancement strategy. Regarding previous reports, the confined space of MOFs could restrict the rotation of the ligands in the shell of nanoclusters and then enhance their luminescence [18–20]. As the inset in Fig. 1a indicates, under exposure to UV radiation, the sole BSA@AuNCs emitted a weak red fluorescence but AuNCs@ZIF-8 emitted an intensive red fluorescence. It should be noted that the blue light in the inset of Fig. 1a, is attributed to scattering spectra at the wavelength of excitation and fluorescence spectra of water and highly diluted aqueous media [21]. The quantum yield (QY) of BSA@AuNCs and AuNCs@ZIF-8 were calculated 1.1% and 10.0%, respectively (fluorescein, QY = 97% as the standard).

gCDs were synthesized via a facile one-step hydrothermal approach. When excited at 400 nm, the gCDs exhibit excitation-independent photoluminescence centered at 520 nm (Fig. 1b). The QY of gCDs was calculated to be 35% using quinine sulfate as a standard sample (54% in 0.1 mol/L). At λex = 400 nm, gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite emitted a well-resolveddual emission fluorescence spectrum with peaks located at 520 and 630 nm (Fig. 1c, blue curve). Based on the location of fluorescence peaks, it is evident that the fluorescence features of gCDs and AuNCs are preserved when confined in MOF. Pure ZIF-8 showed no distinguished fluorescence peak (Fig. 1c, red curve) suggesting that the gCDs and BSA@AuNCs are successfully incorporated into the framework of ZIF-8 and the fluorescence of the gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite arises from gCDs and BSA@AuNCs rather than ZIF-8.

The HR-TEM image (Fig. S1a) demonstrates that the BSA@AuNCs are well dispersed, and their diameters are approximately 5–7 nm. Fig. S1b shows the HR-TEM image of the synthesized gCDs, illustrating that gCDs are uniform spherical nanoparticles with a relatively narrow size distribution from 2 to 4 nm. Also, gCDs are well separated and no apparent aggregation was observed.

The SEM image of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite has been illustrated in Fig. 2 a. As can be seen, the gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite has narrow size distribution and the particle diameters are between 60 and 80 nm. TEM (Fig. 2b) image of the gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite. Based on TEM image, the gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 composite has a rhombic dodecahedron shape with an average size of about 70 nm.

The crystal structures of pure ZIF-8 and gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 were investigated using XRD. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, the XRD patterns of both the ZIF-8 and gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 are matched with that of simulated ZIF-8 suggesting that incorporation of AuNCs and gCDs didn’t affect the crystal growth of ZIF-8. Both of the ZIF-8 and gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 show the reflection at 2θ = 7.3° attributed to a 110 plane suggesting the crystal structures of ZIF-8 and gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 are constructed from 12 [110] facets and show a rhombic dodecahedron-crystals [22]. The BET surface area of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructure was calculated to be 324.8 m2g−1, which is less than the accepted value for ZIF-8 nanocrystals mainly because of the occupation of the cages of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 by gCDs and AuNCs [23].

It was found that CFX remarkably quenched the fluorescence of AuNCs, but it had no effect on the gCDs fluorescence. Therefore, we can design a ratiometric sensor for CFX using AuNCs and gCDs as response and the reference signals, respectively. We also studied the effect of CFX on as-synthesized gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 nanostructure. With the increase of CFX concentration, the fluorescence of AuNCs at 630 nm declined progressively and the emission intensity of gCDs at 520 nm remained almost stable. The ratio of luminescence intensity (F520/F630) showed a certain dependence on CFX concentration. To obtain high sensitivity for CFX, the impact of some experimental parameters including pH and the incubation time, on the ratio of the fluorescence intensity (F520/F630) were carried out. The fluorescence intensity of gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 is stable in the pH range of 6.0–9.0. The effect of pH on the ratio of fluorescence intensity in the presence of CFX (2 ng/mL) was studied in the range of 6.0–9.0 (at 0.5 pH intervals). As can be seen in Fig. S2a, the maximum fluorescence response was obtained at pH 7.5. Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.1 mol/L, pH 7.5) was used throughout the work.

We also investigated the influence of equilibrium time on the fluorescence ratio. As shown in Fig. S2b, in the presence of 2 ng/mL of CFX, the ratio of fluorescence intensity increased rapidly at the initial 3 min. Then, the fluorescence ratio increased slowly and reachedaplateau in 7 min. To make the response completed, 7 min was chosen as the incubation time.

To assess the analytical efficiency of the gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 ratiometric probe for the detection of CFX, the response of the gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 to different concentrations of CFX was studied. As demonstrated in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity at 630 nm gradually quenches with increasing the CFX concentration, while the fluorescence intensity at 520 nm remains constant. Fig. 3b shows the plot of F520/F630 versus CFX concentration, indicating a great linear dependence in the concentration range of 0.1–6 ng/mL. linear curve fitted an equation F520/F630 = 0.1816C + 0.4987 (R2 = 0.9949) (C refer to the concentration of CFX). The detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 0.04 ng/mL CFX.

The analytical performance of some recent optical methods for the detection of CFX can be seen in Table 1 . Compared to other techniques, this sensor showed excellent sensitivity and also has the advantage of being eco-friendly and inexpensive. The excellent sensitivity of this probe was achieved by two features of the sensor. The first feature is ratiometry which due to built-in correction for fluorescence fluctuations, provides unique sensitivity. The second feature is using ZIF-8 as confining scaffold for fluorescent species. Our sensor is very fast with respect to electrochemical and chromatographic methods. Also, this method is facile and don’t require expensive instruments or complicated procedures [4–6].

The strong interactions of CFX with BSA were reported previously in literature [26,27]. The complex formation between CFX and BSA ligands on the surface of AuNCs is the main reason for the fluorescence quenching. Due to complexation between CFX and BSA on the AuNCs surface and the alteration of physiochemical situation of AuNCs, the level of radioactive decay decreases. The intense interaction of BSA and CFX was proved via the remarkable quenching of the BSA fluorescence (centered at 360 nm) in the presence of various concentration of CFX.

To demonstrate the advantages of using MOF scaffold, CFX triggered fluorescence quenching of the sole AuNCs was used for quantification of CFX. Fig. 4 indicates the decrease in the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs and gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 structure in the presence of CFX (2 ng/mL). The quenching of the fluorescence of AuNCs confined in the gCDs/AuNCs@ZIF-8 structure was considerably higher than the decline in the fluorescence of sole AuNCs with the same amount of CFX (63% compared to 94%). This remarkable sensitivity is attributed to the high porosity of ZIF-8 and its high sorption ability that causes the accumulation of the analyte. On the other hand, MOF enhances the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs and provides high sensitivity for the ratiometric sensor. Also, MOFs can provide highly selective sensing privilege by their small pore sizes that prevent specific interfering molecules to get inside.

To investigate the performance of probe for detection of CFX in real food samples, the anti-interference ability of the probe toward potential interfering substances was examined. For this purpose, the response of sensor for CFX (2 ng/mL) in the presence of 100 folds of probable interfering materials such as common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Fe3+) and some biomolecule (Alanine, Glycine, Tryptophan, Glucose, Lactose and Sucrose) were obtained. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the fluorescence ratio (F520/F630) didn't display any noticeable change in the presence of a high concentration of interferences (relative errors are less than 5%). These results revealed that the MOF encapsulation endows the probe with high selectivity.

The feasibility of the as-prepared ratiometric fluorescence probe was evaluated by determining CFX in milk samples. Raw and pasteurized were obtained from the local market. Samples were pretreated as mentioned in section 2.7 and were spiked with various amounts (2, 5, and 10 ng/mL) of CFX, then recovery analyses were carried out. It is worth mentioning that spiked amounts are much lower than FDA allowed MRL value for CFX. The recoveries were in the range of 95.6–105.7%, with RSD of 1.8–3.5% (Table 2 ). These results were satisfying and indicated that the developed CFX sensor was feasible in practical analysis.

Dual emission fluorescence probes, based on gCDs and AuNCs incorporated in a ZIF-8, were designed for the ratiometric detection of CFX. Integration unique features of ZIF-8 and the appealing fluorescence properties of gCDs and AuNCs were exploited for visual read-out, highly sensitive and selective detection of CFX at trace levels. This affordable, reliable, and rapid ratiometric probe can be used as an alternative method for the routine surveillance analysis of CFX residues in milk samples.

Roghayeh Jalili: Experiments, Writing- Original draft preparation. Mahsa Haddad Irani-nezhad: Experiments, Reviewing and Editing. Alireza Khataee: Supervision, Reviewing and Editing. Sang Woo Joo: Characterization of samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is funded by the grant NRF-2019R1A5A8080290 of the National Research Foundation of Korea (S.W.J.).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120089.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Food Agr. Immunol.
